findings.title: No cookie consent banner or cookie consent mechanism
findings.description: The application uses Plausible analytics on the marketing site without a cookie/consent banner to allow users to opt-in or opt-out. GDPR requires explicit, freely given, specific, and informed consent before placing non-essential cookies. The privacy policy discloses analytics usage but there is no mechanism for users to manage consent preferences, withdraw consent, or access a cookie preferences center. Without a functional cookie consent system, the site cannot demonstrate lawful basis for non-essential tracking.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Integrate a GDPR-compliant cookie consent library (e.g., cookie-consent, Cookiebot free tier, or OneTrust basic). 2. Add a cookie banner to all pages that appears before analytics script is executed. 3. Make consent granular: Essential (always enabled), Analytics (opt-in), Marketing (opt-in). 4. Provide a way to withdraw/modify consent in footer or settings. 5. Update privacy policy with specific cookie names, purposes, and retention periods. 6. Load analytics script conditionally only after consent is granted.
findings.evidence: Plausible analytics loaded without consent banner: '<script async src="https://plausible.io/js/pa-zBfbav_s2OcQFGk4H2Rfd.js"></script>' in frontend/privacy.html and marketing pages. No cookie-consent library detected (e.g., OneTrust, Cookiebot, cookie-consent). Marketing HTML has no banner or modal for consent management.
findings.impact: Violation of GDPR Article 7 (conditions for consent) and ePrivacy Directive (requirement for prior consent before placing cookies). EU data protection authorities can issue fines up to €10M or 2% of global turnover, whichever is higher. Users may file complaints with DPAs. Search engines may deprioritize sites without lawful cookie consent.

findings.title: Privacy policy missing critical disclosures required by GDPR Art. 13/14
findings.description: The privacy policies (marketing/src/pages/privacy.astro and frontend/privacy.html) lack several mandatory disclosures under GDPR Articles 13 and 14: (1) No explicit legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'necessary for contract', 'legitimate interest', etc.). (2) No data retention periods specified for specific data types (galleries, comments, access logs). (3) No Data Protection Officer (DPO) contact information if applicable. (4) No explicit breach notification procedures or how data subjects can report a breach. (5) No information about automated decision-making or profiling. (6) No mention of recipients of personal data beyond 'AWS infrastructure' (SES, Plausible, Resend). (7) No explicit right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Rewrite privacy policy to explicitly state legal basis for each processing activity (e.g., 'Client account management is necessary for contract performance', 'Analytics are based on legitimate interest in service improvement'). 2. Add specific retention periods for each data type: access logs (1 year TTL), comments (30 days pending review, then 90 days archived), demo sessions (1 year TTL). 3. List all third parties with explicit links to their privacy policies (Plausible, Resend, AWS). 4. Add section on data subjects' rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, object, automated decision-making). 5. Include DPO contact (if applicable) or responsible person for privacy inquiries. 6. Document your breach notification procedures and provide contact email for breach reports.
findings.evidence: Privacy policies state: 'Gallery data remains until deleted or expired by the operator. Pending comments and operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless longer review is needed.' but do NOT specify: retention for access logs, sub-admin accounts, demo sessions. Email disclosure to Plausible/Resend is mentioned generically but not by name or with link to their privacy policies. No legal basis mentioned for processing client emails (contract? legitimate interest?).
findings.impact: Violation of GDPR Articles 13 and 14 regarding transparency. Data subjects have the right to clear, transparent information about how their data is processed. Lack of detailed disclosures can result in DPA enforcement, administrative fines (€10-20M or 2-4% of turnover), and erosion of user trust.

findings.title: GDPR data subject access (Art. 15) endpoint requires super-admin key, not accessible to data subjects
findings.description: GDPR Article 15 gives individuals the right to obtain confirmation of whether their data is being processed and to receive a copy of that data. The `/admin/gdpr/search` endpoint exists and returns all personal data for an email, but it requires the super-admin X-Admin-Key header. This means individual data subjects cannot self-serve access their data—they must contact the operator (photographer) who then must retrieve the data and manually compile it. The endpoint should be publicly accessible (or behind authentication that any individual can use) so data subjects can exercise their right independently.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a public, unauthenticated endpoint (or one behind email-based verification) that allows individuals to request their data: POST /gdpr/access-request with email. 2. Implement email verification (send link to confirm email ownership). 3. Upon verification, return a full data export (JSON or CSV) including all records from all tables, not just samples. 4. Ensure response time meets GDPR requirement (45 days, extendable by 2 months). 5. Log the request and response delivery. Alternatively, require pre-existing client account login to access /account/export-my-data endpoint.
findings.evidence: gdpr_search() function requires super-admin authentication. Endpoint returns all PII: 'results["tables"]["access_log"] = { "count": len(items), "fields": ["email", "ip", "action", "timestamp", "gallery_id"], "sample": items[:5] }'. Only 5 samples returned, not all data. No public/unauthenticated path to this endpoint documented in API routes.
findings.impact: Non-compliance with GDPR Article 15 right of access. Data subjects have the legal right to request and receive their personal data without excessive delay. Failing to provide an accessible mechanism for this is a violation. DPA can issue an enforcement action requiring remediation. Affected individuals can lodge complaints and seek compensation for material or non-material damage.

findings.title: GDPR data erasure (Art. 17) endpoint requires super-admin key, not data-subject-initiated
findings.description: Similar to GDPR-003, the `/admin/gdpr/erase` endpoint (GDPR Article 17 right to erasure) is only accessible to super-admins via X-Admin-Key header. Data subjects have the right to request deletion of their personal data, but this application forces them to contact the photographer/operator instead of providing a self-service mechanism. While the backend logic correctly identifies and deletes data from multiple tables, the access control prevents individuals from exercising this right independently.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a public/authenticated endpoint for data erasure requests: POST /gdpr/erasure-request with email and reason. 2. Implement email verification (confirmation link sent to email). 3. If reason is 'withdrawal of consent', allow self-service. If reason is 'right to be forgotten', log the request for manual review by operator. 4. After verification, proceed with gdpr_erase() logic. 5. Provide a response indicating what was deleted and timelines. 6. Log all erasure requests with audit trail.
findings.evidence: gdpr_erase() function checks: 'if provided.startswith("demo_"): ...' for super-admin key only. Deletion logic is comprehensive but hidden behind admin-only auth.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 17 violation. Data subjects have the right to erasure under certain conditions (no longer necessary for purpose, withdrawal of consent, unlawful processing, etc.). Not providing a self-service mechanism violates this right and can result in DPA enforcement and fines.

findings.title: Access log retention period unclear and potentially indefinite
findings.description: The privacy policy states: 'Pending comments and operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless longer review is needed.' However, the access_log table in DynamoDB has a TTL attribute (expires_at) but the code does not set a default expiry time when inserting access log records. This means access logs could remain indefinitely unless the operator manually sets an expiry. GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept only as long as necessary. Indefinite or unclear retention violates this principle.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Update access log insertion code to explicitly set expires_at to current_time + 1_year (or operator-configured TTL). 2. Update privacy policy to explicitly state: 'Access logs are retained for 1 year and automatically deleted via DynamoDB TTL.' 3. For sub-admin/demo sessions, confirm default retention is also set and documented. 4. Add configuration option in environment variables to allow customization of retention periods by operator (e.g., LOG_RETENTION_DAYS=365).
findings.evidence: DynamoDB table definition: 'ttl { attribute_name = "expires_at" enabled = true }' enables TTL but code that inserts access_log records does not set expires_at. See auth_view/handler.py where access logs are created but no expiry is set: access_log_table.put_item(Item={...}) without expires_at field.
findings.impact: Indefinite storage of personal data (emails, IPs, activity logs) violates GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation principle. DPA can issue guidance and enforcement actions. Users can claim that their data is processed longer than necessary.

findings.title: Third-party data sharing agreements and DPAs not documented
findings.description: The application uses third-party services (Plausible analytics, Resend email, AWS infrastructure) that may process personal data. GDPR Article 6(1) and Chapter III (Articles 44-49) require that data transfers be documented, and data processing agreements (DPAs) must be in place with processors. The privacy policy mentions these services generically but does not provide links to their privacy policies, confirm DPAs are in place, or address how adequacy decisions are handled. Additionally, if these services store data in the US or other non-EEA jurisdictions, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or other transfer mechanisms must be documented.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Review and confirm DPAs are in place with: AWS (for Lambda, DynamoDB, S3, SES), Plausible (or switch to privacy-friendly alternative like Fathom or Metrical), Resend (if used for transactional emails). 2. Document the legal basis for data transfers (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). 3. Publish links to third-party privacy policies in your privacy policy. 4. Add section: 'Data processors and transfers: Our data is processed by AWS (Standard Contractual Clauses), Plausible (Privacy Shield or SCCs), and Resend (SCCs).' 5. Maintain a Data Processing Inventory documenting all vendors and their contracts.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'Plausible is the only analytics service used on these pages.' but does not link to Plausible's privacy policy or confirm a Data Processing Agreement. Code uses SES and Resend for email: 'ses = boto3.client("ses", region_name=os.environ.get("AWS_REGION", "us-west-2"))' but no documentation of DPA with AWS SES. Resend integration in code not visible in handlers reviewed.
findings.impact: Without documented DPAs and transfer mechanisms, the application cannot legally transfer EU personal data to US-based services. GDPR Articles 28 and 44-49 require this documentation. DPA enforcement can include orders to cease data transfers and fines.

findings.title: Admin API CORS configuration allows all origins (*)
findings.description: The API Gateway CORS configuration in terraform/api_gateway.tf explicitly sets: 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' when responding to admin and auth endpoints. This allows any website to make cross-origin requests to the API from any origin. While the backend still requires authentication (X-Admin-Key or Bearer JWT), the permissive CORS headers increase attack surface: (1) Credential-based attacks can be crafted from any origin. (2) Browser-based CSRF attacks are not protected by CORS. (3) Mistake in authorization logic can be exploited from anywhere. Additionally, the admin handler also sets CORS_ORIGIN from an environment variable with default '*'.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Remove the CORS_ORIGIN environment variable default '*' in lambda/admin/handler.py. Instead, whitelist specific origins: 'ALLOWED_ORIGINS = [os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "")].filter(bool)' or maintain a hardcoded list. 2. Update the respond() function to check if origin is in whitelist before setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. 3. Set CORS_ORIGIN environment variable to the specific gallery domain(s) only. 4. Add X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest check for state-changing requests (POST, DELETE). 5. Test that requests from unauthorized origins receive no CORS headers.
findings.evidence: Terraform: 'allow_origins = [ "https://${local.marketing_domain}", "https://${var.marketing_www_domain}", "https://${local.gallery_domain}" ]' is correctly scoped in API Gateway. However, lambda/admin/handler.py line 454: '"Access-Control-Allow-Origin": os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "*")' sets default to wildcard. This header overrides the API Gateway CORS configuration.
findings.impact: Overly permissive CORS violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 access control principle. It increases risk of credential-stuffing attacks, CSRF, and data theft. While authentication mitigates some risk, the defense-in-depth principle is violated. Auditors will flag this as a security weakness.

findings.title: Access logs and CloudWatch logs include PII (email, IP) without redaction
findings.description: Access log records include raw email and IP addresses in CloudWatch logs: 'access_log_table.put_item(Item={..."email": email..."ip": ip...})'. These logs are queryable and may be retained longer than necessary. Additionally, API Gateway logs in CloudWatch include source IP: '"sourceIp": "$context.identity.sourceIp"'. While audit logging is important, GDPR and ISO 27001 A.8.15 require that logs be minimized for PII and retention be limited. Logs containing PII should be subject to the same retention and deletion policies as other personal data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Update access_log insertion to anonymize or hash email/IP: use hashlib.sha256(email.encode()).hexdigest() instead of raw email. 2. For rate limiting and abuse detection that require IPs, hash the IP as well. 3. Set CloudWatch log retention to match data retention policy (1 year for access logs, 30 days for debug logs). 4. Add a field to distinguish log types: 'type': 'access_attempt', 'failed_auth', 'api_error' to allow filtering sensitive logs. 5. Implement log scrubbing to redact PII from error messages before they're logged.
findings.evidence: API Gateway logs include sourceIp. Access log DynamoDB table stores email and IP without aggregation or anonymization. No explicit log retention policy mentioned beyond CloudWatch default (depends on log group retention settings).
findings.impact: GDPR Article 5 and 6 require that personal data processing be minimized and lawful. Storing PII in audit logs without clear retention policy can violate storage limitation (Art. 5(1)(e)). If logs are breached, it exposes email and IP data. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires audit logs to be protected and retention-controlled.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 — Logging and Audit Trails

findings.title: Rate limiting not enforced on GDPR endpoints (/admin/gdpr/search, /admin/gdpr/erase)
findings.description: The /admin/gdpr/search and /admin/gdpr/erase endpoints require super-admin key authentication but do not have per-endpoint rate limiting beyond the global WAF (1000 req / 5 min per IP) and API Gateway defaults (100 req/sec, 200 burst). An attacker with the super-admin key (or who obtains it via compromise) could invoke these endpoints repeatedly to enumerate all data subjects in the system or to trigger bulk deletion. Additionally, the ratelimit_table in DynamoDB has server_side_encryption enabled but no point_in_time_recovery, which could allow data loss in case of attack or error.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add rate limiting to GDPR endpoints: check ratelimit_table before processing, increment counter per email/IP. E.g., 'max 5 access requests per email per hour', 'max 2 erasure requests per email per 24 hours'. 2. Return 429 Too Many Requests if limit exceeded. 3. Enable point_in_time_recovery on ratelimit_table: 'point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }' in terraform/dynamodb.tf. 4. Log all GDPR requests with detailed audit trail (who, when, what data, outcome). 5. Consider requiring explicit confirmation (second factor) for erasure requests.
findings.evidence: gdpr_search() and gdpr_erase() functions do not check a local rate limit cache. They perform full table scans: '_scan_all(access_log_table, ...)' which could be resource-expensive. No explicit rate limiting logic or token bucket per function.
findings.impact: Without rate limiting on sensitive operations, the system is vulnerable to abuse. An attacker with admin key could exhaust API quotas, enumerate all users, or trigger mass deletions. This violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 (access control) and increases risk of data loss or service disruption.

findings.description: The demo_key validation in lambda/admin/handler.py uses a full table scan without pagination, which could allow a slow DoS attack and exposes all demo sessions to be enumerated if demo_key values are guessable.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) on demo_sessions_table with demo_key as hash key: 'global_secondary_index { name = "demo_key-index", hash_key = "demo_key" }'. 2. Query this index instead of full scan: 'demo_sessions_table.query(IndexName="demo_key-index", KeyConditionExpression=Key("demo_key").eq(key))'. 3. Set Limit=1 on the query since demo_key should be unique.
findings.evidence: Demo key validation: 'items = _scan_all(demo_sessions_table, FilterExpression=Attr("demo_key").eq(key))'. _scan_all() fetches all items without limit. A table with many demo sessions could cause high latency and cost.
findings.impact: Inefficient data access patterns consume resources and could be exploited for DoS. This violates ISO 27001 A.5.15 (access control) principle of minimizing resource consumption for security operations.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.25 — Input Validation and Sanitization

findings.title: CloudFront signed URLs use SHA1 for signing (deprecated cryptographic hash)
findings.description: CloudFront signed URL generation uses SHA1 (PKCS#1 v1.5 padding) to sign URLs: 'signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())'. SHA1 is deprecated and no longer recommended for cryptographic purposes due to collision vulnerabilities. While CloudFront still supports SHA1 and collisions are difficult to produce in this specific context (signing structured policy documents), using a deprecated algorithm violates ISO 27001 A.8.3 cryptographic controls best practice. This is also noted in pyproject.toml as an exception: 'S303 — SHA1 required by CloudFront signed URLs'.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Monitor CloudFront and AWS documentation for support of SHA256 signing (may require changes to CloudFront key pair type or region support). 2. If SHA256 becomes available, migrate to it: 'hashes.SHA256()' instead of 'hashes.SHA1()'. 3. For now, document this as a 'known limitation' due to CloudFront API constraints and confirm with AWS that SHA1 is secure enough for this use case. 4. Add a note to security.astro or security documentation explaining the trade-off.
findings.evidence: auth_view/handler.py: 'signature = private_key.sign(policy.encode(), padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA1())'. pyproject.toml: 'S303 # SHA1 for CF signing' indicates acknowledgment of this limitation.
findings.impact: Low immediate risk since CloudFront's implementation is robust and collision attacks are impractical for this use case. However, continued use of SHA1 violates ISO 27001 best practices and modern cryptographic standards. EU and international bodies recommend phasing out SHA1 entirely. Auditors will flag this as a technical debt.

findings.title: No explicit requirement for TLS 1.3 or minimum version enforcement
findings.description: While HSTS and signed URLs enforce HTTPS, there is no explicit minimum TLS version requirement in the CloudFront or API Gateway configuration. The default AWS behavior should be TLS 1.2+, but best practice (ISO 27001 A.8.24) is to explicitly require TLS 1.3 or at minimum TLS 1.2, and disable older versions. Additionally, no explicit mention of certificate pinning for mobile clients (if any).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. In CloudFront distribution resource, add: 'minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021-06"' (or TLSv1.3 if available). 2. In API Gateway, confirm default TLS version is 1.2+ via AWS documentation or API. 3. Consider adding Security-Headers HPKP (Public Key Pinning) header if mobile clients are used. 4. Test with nmap or testssl.sh to confirm TLS 1.0/1.1 are disabled.
findings.evidence: No minimum_protocol_version set in CloudFront distribution. No tls_version requirement in API Gateway.
findings.impact: Low risk due to AWS defaults being secure. However, explicitly configuring minimum TLS version demonstrates compliance with ISO 27001 and defense-in-depth. Without explicit configuration, any AWS default change could reduce security.

findings.title: No comprehensive audit trail for administrator actions
findings.description: The admin Lambda logs some operations (create gallery, delete gallery, GDPR actions) to CloudWatch, but there is no centralized audit trail that explicitly tracks all admin actions with sufficient detail (who, what, when, where, result). For example, gallery updates, photo approval/rejection, comment moderation, and team member creation are not explicitly logged with user identity and timestamp.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a dedicated audit_log function that logs all admin actions: 'def audit_log(action, resource_id, user_id, result, details={})'. 2. Call this function for: create/update/delete gallery, approve/reject photo, moderate comment, create/modify sub-admin, generate tokens. 3. Store audit logs in a separate immutable table or CloudWatch log group with long retention (3-7 years). 4. Include fields: timestamp, action, resource_id, user_id, IP, result (success/failure), details. 5. Implement log aggregation and alerting for suspicious patterns (e.g., multiple failed auth attempts, mass deletions).
findings.evidence: Some log entries: 'logger.info("Created gallery %s for client: %s", gallery_id, client_name)' but not all operations are logged. Gallery photo updates, comment moderation, sub-admin creation have no explicit logging visible in reviewed sections.
findings.impact: Low immediate risk, but lack of comprehensive audit trail weakens compliance posture. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires that all security-relevant events be logged. If a data breach occurs, incomplete audit logs hinder incident investigation and may violate GDPR Article 33 breach notification requirements.

findings.title: No documented incident response or breach notification procedure
findings.description: There is no documented incident response plan, breach notification procedure, or runbook for handling security incidents. The security.astro page mentions a security contact email but does not provide procedures for how incidents are triaged, investigated, or how users are notified in case of a breach. GDPR Article 33 requires notification to supervisory authorities within 72 hours of discovering a personal data breach.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create docs/INCIDENT-RESPONSE.md documenting: (a) How security issues are reported (email, responsible person). (b) Initial triage and severity assessment. (c) Investigation steps and forensic procedures. (d) Notification timelines (internal: immediate, DPA: 72 hours, users: without undue delay). (e) Communication templates for affected users. (f) Remediation and recovery steps. 2. Create docs/BREACH-NOTIFICATION.md with breach notification template that includes: nature of breach, categories of personal data, likely consequences, measures taken to mitigate. 3. Set up CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activities (high rate of deletions, auth failures, WAF blocks) and route them to the security team via SNS.
findings.evidence: Security page: 'Report security concerns to security@codyjo.com' but no procedures documented. No incident response playbook found in codebase. No references to breach notification templates or timelines.
findings.impact: GDPR Article 33 requires breach notification within 72 hours. Without a documented procedure, the likelihood of timely notification is reduced. This can result in regulatory fines and legal liability. ISO 27001 A.5.24 requires incident response planning.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documented
findings.description: GDPR Article 35 requires that a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) be conducted for processing that is 'likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons'. Analogify processes personal photos, email addresses, IP addresses, and activity data, and may involve automated processing (e.g., photo analysis by ChromaHaus). This is exactly the type of processing that typically requires a DPIA. There is no evidence of a DPIA being conducted or documented.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Conduct a DPIA following the EDPB guidelines (EDPB Template or ISO/IEC 27550). 2. Document processing activities: (a) Identification of data controller/processor. (b) Purposes of processing. (c) Legitimate interest assessment (if applicable). (d) Categories of data subjects and personal data. (e) Risk assessment: identify risks to individuals' rights and freedoms. (f) Mitigation measures (technical and organizational). (g) Necessity and proportionality assessment. 3. Store the DPIA in docs/DPIA.md and reference it in the privacy policy. 4. If risks are identified as high, consult with supervisory authority per Article 36.
findings.evidence: No docs/DPIA.md or similar file found in codebase. Recital 75 of GDPR guidance suggests DPIAs for: large-scale processing of special categories, systematic monitoring, automated decision-making. ChromaHaus photo analysis could be considered automated processing (though not decision-making).
findings.impact: GDPR Article 35 violation. Failure to conduct a DPIA when required can result in regulatory guidance, enforcement orders, and fines (€10-20M or up to 4% of global turnover). A DPIA is a demonstration of compliance and helps identify and mitigate risks.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 35 — Data Protection Impact Assessment

findings.title: No Data Protection Officer (DPO) role or contact information
findings.description: GDPR Article 37 requires certain organizations to designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO). While Analogify is likely a small organization and may be exempt, the privacy policy does not clarify whether a DPO is designated or who the responsible person is for privacy inquiries. This creates ambiguity for individuals trying to exercise their rights or contact the organization about privacy concerns.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Determine if Analogify meets DPO requirement criteria under GDPR Article 37(1) (public authority, core activity is systematic monitoring, large-scale processing of special categories). 2. If exempt, add to privacy policy: 'A Data Protection Officer is not required under GDPR Article 37. Privacy inquiries can be directed to: cody@codyjo.com'. 3. If DPO is required or voluntarily designated, add to privacy policy: 'Our Data Protection Officer is [Name] ([email])' and ensure DPO contact is publicly available.
findings.evidence: Privacy contact: 'Questions or privacy requests can be sent to cody@codyjo.com' but no mention of whether Cody is a DPO or the responsible person.
findings.impact: Low risk if organization is exempt from DPO requirement (fewer than 250 employees, non-systematic monitoring, not processing special categories at scale). However, absence of clarity creates regulatory risk and user confusion. Auditors may flag this as incomplete governance.

findings.title: Age verification not applicable to this project
findings.description: Analogify Studio is a B2B photographer gallery delivery platform. It does not serve content directly to end consumers, nor does it host adult content, social media functionality, or content restricted by age. The platform is designed for photographers to deliver client galleries, which are access-controlled and requires gallery owner authentication. Individual photographers are responsible for compliance with age verification laws if they serve minors. The platform itself does not need to implement age verification gates.
findings.fix_suggestion: No action required. However, if in future the platform adds: (1) a public gallery directory with uncontrolled access, (2) social media features, (3) age-restricted content delivery, or (4) consumer-facing apps (Analogify Zen backend), age verification may become applicable.
findings.evidence: Platform architecture: photographers authenticate with admin panel, create galleries, clients authenticate via email-based entry. No public content, no age-restricted content, no social features, no user-to-user interaction beyond moderated comments. Analogify Zen (consumer product) is marketing-only and has no backend.
findings.impact: No legal risk. Age verification requirements do not apply to this project type.
findings.regulation: US State Laws (Louisiana, Utah, Texas, Virginia, Arkansas, Montana, California), UK Online Safety Act

findings.title: Secrets and API keys stored in plaintext in configuration files
findings.description: The backoffice.yaml configuration file (intentionally gitignored) stores sensitive values in plaintext: AWS access/secret keys, CloudFront distribution IDs, API keys, and database credentials. If this file is compromised, an attacker gains full access to AWS infrastructure, dashboards, and potentially target repositories. No encryption at rest is applied to configuration data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Integrate AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault for secrets storage. 2. Modify backoffice/config.py to fetch sensitive values from the secrets manager at startup. 3. Use environment variable references in backoffice.yaml for sensitive fields (e.g., api_key: "${API_KEY}"). 4. Document the secrets management approach in setup instructions. 5. Add pre-commit hook to prevent accidental commits of plaintext secrets.
findings.evidence: config/backoffice.example.yaml shows: api_key: "", aws region/bucket/distribution credentials all stored as plaintext YAML. The config loader (backoffice/config.py) reads these values directly into dataclasses with no decryption step.
findings.impact: If configuration is compromised, unauthorized access to audit findings and target infrastructure violates ISO 27001 control A.8.2 and A.8.24 requirements. Potential exposure of AWS credentials could lead to infrastructure compromise, data theft, and financial liability (AWS abuse).

findings.title: No enforcement of HTTPS/TLS for API server
findings.description: The api_server.py and server.py use Python's http.server (HTTP only). There is no built-in TLS/SSL support. If deployed directly (not behind a reverse proxy with TLS termination), all API traffic is sent in plaintext, exposing API keys, findings data, and scan requests.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Document that the API server MUST be deployed behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Apache, AWS ALB) with TLS termination. 2. Alternatively, add ssl.wrap_socket() context to api_server.py if direct HTTPS is needed. 3. Add a startup check that logs a WARNING if api_key is configured but no reverse proxy is detected. 4. Update deployment documentation to include HTTPS-required notation. 5. Add security headers (HSTS, CSP) in the reverse proxy configuration.
findings.evidence: api_server.py line 1 uses http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler without ssl module wrapper. No SSL context creation or certificate loading. README deployment section does not explicitly require reverse proxy with HTTPS.
findings.impact: API keys and audit findings transmitted over plaintext HTTP violate ISO 27001 A.8.24 encryption in transit requirements. Man-in-the-middle attacks could intercept API keys or manipulate findings.

findings.title: No rate limiting on API endpoints
findings.description: The API server (api_server.py) accepts requests without rate limiting. An attacker can brute-force API keys, enumerate scan endpoints, or trigger denial-of-service by spawning unlimited concurrent agent jobs (each consuming CPU/memory).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement per-IP rate limiting using a library like python-ratelimit or Flask-Limiter (even though this is a raw http.server). 2. Add a simple in-memory token bucket or sliding window counter keyed by request origin. 3. Return HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) when rate limit is exceeded. 4. Recommend deploying behind a reverse proxy with rate limiting (nginx limit_req, AWS WAF). 5. Document rate limit thresholds in the API documentation.
findings.evidence: do_POST() method accepts any request and calls run_agent() without checking request rate. No per-IP or per-key rate limiting. No request queuing or backpressure mechanism.
findings.impact: Lack of rate limiting enables brute-force attacks against API keys and denial-of-service attacks, violating ISO 27001 A.8.2 authentication controls and A.8.1 access control principles.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.2 (User Authentication Management) - Rate Limiting

findings.title: Insufficient audit logging of security-relevant events
findings.description: While backoffice/log_config.py provides structured logging, security-relevant events are not comprehensively logged: failed API authentication attempts are not explicitly logged, API key usage is not tracked, unauthorized endpoint access is not recorded, and data access patterns are not logged. Logs may also contain exception stacktraces with sensitive information (file paths, internal structure).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add explicit logging for authentication failures: log failed API key attempts with source IP and timestamp. 2. Log all API requests with method, path, source IP, API key ID (not value), status code, and response size. 3. Add separate audit log file (audit.log) with immutable append-only semantics. 4. Sanitize exception logs to avoid exposing file paths, config values, or internal structure. 5. Implement log retention and rotation policies.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _check_auth() returns True/False but does not log failed auth attempts. No audit trail of which API keys accessed which endpoints. No request/response logging in APIHandler.
findings.impact: Lack of comprehensive audit logs prevents detection of unauthorized access, API key compromise, or data tampering. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.16 requirements for logging and monitoring.

findings.title: No API key rotation mechanism
findings.description: API keys are stored in backoffice.yaml and read at startup. There is no rotation schedule, no expiration policy, and no mechanism to revoke compromised keys. If a key is leaked, the only remedy is manual file editing and service restart.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement API key rotation: support multiple active keys with expiration dates. 2. Move keys to a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). 3. Add --rotate-key command to CLI to generate new keys. 4. Log all key rotations and revocations. 5. Document a key rotation policy (e.g., rotate every 90 days).
findings.evidence: api_key stored as static string in config. APIHandler._check_auth() compares against a single key. No key versioning, rotation, or expiration.
findings.impact: Lack of key rotation increases the window of exposure if a key is compromised. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.2 principles of timely access control updates.

findings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy and security headers on dashboard
findings.description: Dashboard HTML files (e.g., dashboard/index.html) include a basic CSP header but it allows unsafe-inline for scripts and styles. This permits XSS attacks if any user-controlled data (e.g., findings from audited repos) is displayed without proper escaping. Additionally, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, and cache-control headers are missing.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Upgrade CSP to remove 'unsafe-inline' for scripts: use nonce-based or hash-based script execution. 2. Move inline styles to external stylesheets. 3. Add X-Frame-Options: DENY to prevent clickjacking. 4. Add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to prevent MIME sniffing. 5. Add Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. 6. Add Permissions-Policy to disable sensitive browser features (geolocation, camera, microphone). 7. For sensitive pages, add Cache-Control: no-store, private.
findings.evidence: index.html: CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for script-src and style-src. This allows inline event handlers and style attributes, enabling XSS. Connect-src is 'self' only, which is good.
findings.impact: XSS vulnerabilities via unsafe CSP allow attackers to steal API keys, session cookies, or inject malicious content into dashboards. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.9 security headers and A.8.25 output encoding principles.

findings.title: Insufficient input validation on TARGET parameter in agent scripts
findings.description: The api_server.py and shell scripts accept a 'target' parameter from the request body. While Python's subprocess.run() with a list avoids shell injection, the target path is not validated against a whitelist. An attacker could potentially specify an arbitrary filesystem path (e.g., /etc/passwd) as a target, causing unexpected behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Validate that the supplied target name matches one of the configured targets in backoffice.yaml. 2. Reject any target path that is not explicitly configured. 3. Add an explicit whitelist check before calling run_agent(). 4. Log rejected scan attempts with the supplied target and source IP.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _handle_scan() reads target from POST body and passes to run_agent(). Target is resolved via resolve_target() but the resolution is permissive: any existing directory path is accepted. No whitelist check against configured targets.
findings.impact: Arbitrary path traversal could expose audit findings on unintended repos or trigger scans on sensitive directories, violating ISO 27001 A.8.26 input validation controls.

findings.title: CORS configuration could be overly permissive if misconfigured
findings.description: api_server.py supports wildcard CORS origin ('*' in allowed_origins). While the default config (backoffice.example.yaml) specifies legitimate origins, a misconfigured deployment could expose the API to requests from any origin, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Update backoffice/api_server.py to reject '*' as an allowed origin (comment out or remove the wildcard check). 2. Document that allowed_origins must be an explicit list of trusted domains. 3. Add a validation check during config loading to reject wildcard origins and log a warning. 4. Recommend deploying with specific, domain-restricted CORS origins only.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _cors_headers() checks: if '*' in allowed or origin in allowed. This allows the config to specify '*' as an allowed origin, which is overly permissive for a sensitive API.
findings.impact: Overly permissive CORS enables cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks where a malicious website triggers requests to the back-office API on behalf of an authenticated user. Violates ISO 27001 A.8.9 access control principles.

findings.title: No explicit request body size limits
findings.description: The api_server.py and server.py do not set maximum request body sizes. An attacker could send arbitrarily large POST payloads to exhaust server memory or trigger denial-of-service conditions.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Set a maximum request body size (e.g., 10MB). 2. In _read_body(), check if Content-Length > MAX_BODY_SIZE and return 413 Payload Too Large. 3. Document the maximum payload size in API documentation. 4. Recommend deploying behind a reverse proxy (nginx, ALB) with request size limits enforced at the perimeter.
findings.evidence: api_server.py _read_body() reads self.rfile.read(length) without checking if length exceeds a reasonable limit (e.g., 1MB). Python's http.server does not enforce a default limit.
findings.impact: Lack of request size limits enables denial-of-service attacks that consume memory and CPU, violating ISO 27001 A.8.3 network security controls.

gdpr.reasoning: Back Office is an internal tool that does not collect, process, or store personal data of end users. It is not subject to GDPR. However, when Back Office audits other projects that process personal data, those projects must comply with GDPR separately.

iso27001.reasoning: Back Office implements basic security controls (API key auth, HMAC timing-safe comparison, structured logging) but lacks critical controls around secrets management, encryption at rest, request validation, and rate limiting.

age_verification.reasoning: Age verification is not applicable. Back Office is an internal tool for developers and does not serve content to end users, minors, or any protected group.

findings.title: Registration consent is required in the UI but not recorded server-side
findings.description: The registration form requires a privacy-policy checkbox, but the submitted payload contains only name, email, password, and preferredVersion, and the backend profile record stores no consent timestamp, policy version, or consent scope. That leaves the service unable to evidence that consent was freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add explicit consent fields to the register payload, store consent scope, timestamp, and policy/version identifiers in DynamoDB, and expose withdrawal/preference management for any optional processing tied to consent.
findings.evidence: The checkbox at lines 178-183 is mandatory, but the POST body at lines 71-76 omits any consent fields. The backend profile written in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 416-431 stores email, name, passwordHash, salt, preferredVersion, emailVerified, and createdAt only.
findings.impact: If Selah relies on consent for account creation disclosures, analytics, or other optional processing, it cannot demonstrate valid consent during a regulator inquiry or user complaint, creating exposure to corrective orders and administrative fines.

findings.title: Third-party analytics loads before any consent or preference flow
findings.description: The root layout injects Plausible analytics for every visitor as soon as the app loads. The repository contains no consent banner, analytics preference center, or conditional loading path tied to user choice. Even for cookieless analytics, this creates a GDPR transparency and lawful-basis gap unless the operator has documented and disclosed a defensible alternative basis outside the codebase.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement a consent/preferences layer for analytics and gate Plausible script injection on that preference, or remove third-party analytics for regulated users and update privacy disclosures accordingly.
findings.evidence: layout.tsx lines 66-67 load plausible.io globally. A repo-wide search found no cookie banner or consent-management implementation under src/app, src/components, or src/lib.
findings.impact: Processing analytics data for EEA users without a demonstrable lawful basis or clear opt-out/consent workflow can trigger complaints, orders to stop processing, and regulator scrutiny over third-party transfers and transparency.

findings.title: The backend supports account deletion, but the app exposes no user-facing erasure flow
findings.description: The API provides an authenticated DELETE /api/account route that removes account data, but the Settings page exposes sign-out, backup, import/export, and password-change controls only. There is no in-product delete-account or erase-my-data path, which makes the right to erasure materially harder to exercise.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add a delete-account flow in Settings that requires password re-entry, calls DELETE /api/account, clears local data, and confirms what data will be removed.
findings.evidence: The account UI at lines 223-245 shows profile details and a sign-out button only. The deletion endpoint exists in lambda/api/index.mjs at lines 363 and 847-905, but there is no client-side call to /api/account in the repository.
findings.impact: Users may be unable to exercise Article 17 rights without manual support intervention, increasing complaint risk and making statutory response timelines harder to meet.

findings.title: No retention limit is applied to persisted account data
findings.description: The DynamoDB table supports TTL only through the expiresAt attribute, but user profile, study, journal, bookmark, and daily records are written without any retention field. The repo contains no cleanup job or retention-policy implementation for personal data stored in the account backend.
findings.fix_suggestion: Define retention periods by data type, store expiry metadata where appropriate, and add scheduled purge logic or lifecycle jobs. This requires business and legal input before code changes.
findings.evidence: User content is stored in lambda/api/index.mjs lines 1001-1015, 1092-1130, 1183-1195, and 1260-1271 without expiresAt. terraform/dynamodb.tf lines 38-41 enable TTL only for records that explicitly include expiresAt.
findings.impact: Indefinite retention of personal data without a documented necessity basis can violate the storage-limitation principle and widen exposure if a breach or DSAR occurs.

findings.title: Bearer tokens and user profile data are persisted in localStorage
findings.description: Authentication tokens, user profile data, and cached encryption metadata are stored in browser localStorage. Because localStorage is readable by any injected JavaScript, a future XSS defect or compromised third-party script would expose active sessions and user identifiers. This is weaker than an HttpOnly session-cookie model for a production consumer app processing account and journal data.
findings.fix_suggestion: Move session handling to Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite cookies or another server-managed session mechanism, and keep only strictly necessary non-sensitive state in web storage.
findings.evidence: auth-context.tsx stores bible_token, bible_user, and selah_enc_cache in localStorage at lines 19-21, 25-31, 49-56, and 62-71. StudyChat also reads bible_token from localStorage at src/components/StudyChat.tsx lines 136-143.
findings.impact: Session theft can lead to unauthorized access to personal data, triggering incident-response obligations, account compromise, and possible breach-notification exposure under both ISO-aligned controls and privacy law.

findings.title: Account backup uploads journal content in plaintext when the encryption key is unavailable
findings.description: The account-backup path encrypts journals only when a master key is present. If the user is signed in but the in-memory key is unavailable, the backup routine sends the raw journal entry to the API, and the backend stores plaintext content. That defeats the stated zero-knowledge journal design for a common recovery state.
findings.fix_suggestion: Block journal backup until the journal key is unlocked, or split backup so journals are skipped with a clear warning unless encryption is active. On the backend, reject plaintext journal writes for accounts that have encryption enabled.
findings.evidence: account-sync.ts lines 107-115 send the unmodified journal entry when masterKey is falsy. Settings calls backupLocalDataToAccount(undefined, masterKey) at src/app/settings/page.tsx lines 139-152, so a signed-in user can trigger backup even when masterKey is null.
findings.impact: Sensitive spiritual reflections and prayer-journal content may be stored unencrypted in the backend, increasing breach impact and undermining security assurances made to users.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe inline and eval execution
findings.description: The CloudFront security headers policy sets a CSP, but it permits both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. Those exceptions materially weaken XSS defenses, especially because the frontend also stores bearer tokens in browser storage and loads third-party scripts.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, migrate any inline bootstrapping to nonce- or hash-based scripts, and confirm the build/runtime path works without eval-dependent tooling in production.
findings.evidence: The CSP at terraform/cloudfront.tf lines 218-220 is: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io.
findings.impact: A weaker CSP increases the likelihood that an injection flaw becomes a real account-compromise incident, which raises security and breach-response risk.

findings.title: Privacy policy missing data retention period, DPO contact, and specific lawful basis
findings.description: The privacy policy at /privacy.astro discloses data collection and third parties but omits: (1) specific retention period for contact form data, (2) Data Protection Officer contact information, (3) explicit lawful basis per processing activity (Article 6), (4) breach notification procedures. GDPR Articles 13-14 require complete transparency at the point of data collection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand privacy policy to include: (1) 'Contact form data is retained for [30-90] days or until inquiry is resolved, whichever is longer, then deleted.' (2) 'We process contact data on the basis of [consent given via checkbox / legitimate interest in responding to inquiries].' (3) DPO contact if applicable, or clarify no separate DPO is appointed. (4) 'Suspected breaches are reported to [supervisory authority] within 72 hours. Contact privacy@codyjo.com for security incidents.'
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states general purpose ('to respond to that request') but not: retention duration for contact submissions, lawful basis selection (consent vs. legitimate interest), DPO email if applicable, or breach notification procedure. No mention of retention policy beyond generic 'request-based deletion'.
findings.impact: Incomplete privacy notices violate GDPR Articles 13-14 and can trigger enforcement fines up to €20M or 4% of global turnover. Data subjects can claim damages for non-transparent processing.

findings.title: No documented mechanism for users to exercise data subject rights (access, deletion, portability)
findings.description: Privacy policy states users can request access, correction, or deletion, but provides no email address, contact form, or API endpoint for submitting such requests. GDPR Articles 15-22 require controllers to establish a practical mechanism for data subjects to exercise their rights.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add to privacy policy: 'To exercise your data subject rights, email privacy@codyjo.com with your request. Requests for access will return contact form submissions, timestamps, and consent records within 30 days. Requests for deletion will remove all personal data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'
findings.evidence: Lines 117-121 mention rights exist but offer no submission mechanism. No privacy@codyjo.com email address, dedicated rights-request endpoint, or documented procedure for processing access/deletion requests is provided.
findings.impact: GDPR requires controllers to respond to access requests within 30 days. Lack of a documented mechanism may be interpreted as failure to facilitate rights exercise. Fines up to €10M or 2% of turnover can apply.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 15-22 — Data Subject Rights Mechanism

findings.title: No documented Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Plausible Analytics processor
findings.description: Privacy policy discloses Plausible but does not confirm a signed Data Processing Agreement. GDPR Article 28 requires a DPA when a processor handles personal data on behalf of the controller. Plausible typically offers a standard DPA, but lack of documented agreement creates compliance risk.
findings.fix_suggestion: Verify Plausible DPA is in place (typically included in their standard terms). Update privacy policy to state: 'Plausible is our data processor under a Data Processing Agreement that complies with GDPR Article 28. Plausible does not process personal data for any purpose other than providing analytics services to us.'
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists Plausible with a data policy link but does not state 'We have executed a Data Processing Agreement with Plausible' or reference a DPA addendum. No evidence of DPA in the repository.
findings.impact: Absence of documented DPA is a direct GDPR Article 28 violation. Fines up to €10M or 2% of turnover apply. If Plausible's default terms include a DPA, lack of acknowledgment still creates audit risk.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 28 — Data Processing Agreement for Plausible

findings.title: No documented legal mechanism for US data transfers (Schrems II compliance)
findings.description: Contact form data is sent to AWS Lambda in us-west-2. Privacy policy mentions US hosting but does not reference Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), adequacy decisions, or equivalent safeguards required by GDPR Articles 44-49. Post-Schrems II, inadequate transfer mechanisms expose the controller to enforcement action.
findings.fix_suggestion: Confirm AWS Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are in place via AWS Data Processing Addendum. Update privacy policy: 'Personal data is transferred to the United States under Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) that ensure adequate safeguards. You may request details of the transfer mechanism by contacting privacy@codyjo.com.'
findings.evidence: API endpoint at line 317: https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact points to US infrastructure. Privacy policy (line 43) states 'Infrastructure is primarily hosted in the United States' but does not reference SCCs, adequacy decisions, or supplementary measures.
findings.impact: Without documented legal transfer mechanisms, EU personal data transfer to the US violates GDPR. Recent Schrems II ruling emphasizes strict requirements. Fines up to €20M or 4% of turnover apply. Irish DPC has taken enforcement action on US transfer gaps.

findings.title: No clear mechanism for users to withdraw contact form consent
findings.description: Contact form requires consent checkbox but privacy policy does not explain how to withdraw consent or what happens to data upon withdrawal. GDPR Article 7(3) requires withdrawal to be as easy as giving consent.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy to include: 'You can withdraw your consent at any time by contacting privacy@codyjo.com. Upon withdrawal, we will delete your contact form data unless we have a legal obligation to retain it.'
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox at line 280 but no explanation in privacy policy on how to withdraw consent, revoke permission, or request data deletion after submission. No contact method provided for withdrawal requests.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 7(3) requires transparent withdrawal mechanism. Lack of clarity may trigger regulator complaints.

findings.title: CSP header contains 'unsafe-inline' for scripts, weakening XSS protection
findings.description: Content-Security-Policy header in Base.astro line 66 includes script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io. The 'unsafe-inline' directive defeats XSS protection by allowing arbitrary inline script execution without nonce or hash validation. This significantly weakens browser-enforced security for a site collecting contact data.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src and use nonces: (1) Add a nonce to Plausible script tag (line 123). (2) Astro should support automatic nonce generation. (3) Update CSP to: script-src 'self' 'nonce-[generated]' https://plausible.io;. (4) Move inline Plausible init code to external file or apply nonce.
findings.evidence: CSP meta tag: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io;" The 'unsafe-inline' allows inline <script> tags to execute without restrictions, nullifying CSP's XSS mitigation value.
findings.impact: XSS vulnerabilities on a form collecting contact data could lead to credential theft or data exfiltration, violating GDPR Article 32 (security measures) and ISO 27001 controls. Demonstrated XSS leading to breach could trigger enforcement.

findings.title: Missing HTTP security headers: X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy
findings.description: Site does not explicitly configure several important HTTP security headers to prevent clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, referrer leakage, and browser feature abuse. While CloudFront may apply defaults, relying on implicit CDN behavior rather than explicit configuration is a compliance gap.
findings.fix_suggestion: Configure CloudFront response headers policy or Astro middleware to set: (1) X-Frame-Options: DENY (prevent clickjacking). (2) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME-sniffing). (3) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (limit referrer leakage). (4) Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=() (restrict browser features).
findings.evidence: CSP is set via meta tag (line 66) but no X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy headers are configured. These should be set at the HTTP response level (CloudFront policy or middleware).
findings.impact: Missing security headers increase attack surface for clickjacking and data leakage. If these enable a breach, GDPR Article 32 and ISO 27001 controls are undermined.

findings.title: Contact form client-side validation only; server-side validation not visible
findings.description: Contact form implements client-side validation (email regex, required fields, length limits) but the AWS Lambda backend code is not in the repository. Server-side validation cannot be verified, creating risk of malformed or injection-attack payloads being stored.
findings.fix_suggestion: Verify that the AWS Lambda function implements server-side validation: (1) Email format (robust regex or validation library). (2) Maximum length checks. (3) Required field validation. (4) Character restrictions (reject control characters, unusual Unicode). (5) XSS prevention (escape HTML entities before storage). Return 400/422 if validation fails.
findings.evidence: Email validation at line 338-342 uses client-side regex. Lambda endpoint at ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact is external and not auditable from the repository.
findings.impact: Client-side validation can be bypassed. Without confirmed server-side validation, injection attacks or malformed data could compromise data integrity or enable XSS if data is later reflected.

findings.title: Contact API endpoint lacks documented authentication or authorization
findings.description: Contact form submits to AWS API Gateway endpoint with no visible authentication (no API key, bearer token, or OAuth). While honeypot and rate-limiting provide basic protection, a public endpoint without authentication can be abused for spam, DoS, or malicious data injection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement API authentication: (1) Add API key validation in Lambda (check x-api-key header against environment variable). (2) Or use AWS API Gateway Lambda authorizer with IAM role. (3) Verify CORS is restricted to https://www.codyjo.com origin. (4) Confirm 429 rate-limiting is in place (already implemented per code).
findings.evidence: Fetch request at line 362 sends JSON with Content-Type header only; no Authorization header or API key visible. No evidence of API Gateway IAM authorization or Lambda authorization logic in the repository.
findings.impact: Unprotected API endpoints risk abuse, spam flooding, or data injection. If attackers inject malicious content or cause service degradation, it undermines ISO 27001 A.8.2 access controls and availability.

findings.title: No documented retention period for server and CDN logs
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'server-side and CDN request logs' but does not specify retention duration. Log retention policy is essential for audit trails and incident investigation per ISO 27001 A.5.11.
findings.fix_suggestion: Update privacy policy: 'Server and CDN logs are retained for 90 days in accordance with AWS CloudFront default retention and used for security investigation and troubleshooting. Logs are not shared with third parties unless required by law.'
findings.evidence: Lines 67-72 describe logs but state only that they are 'used for site reliability, abuse prevention, and troubleshooting' without specifying retention period (e.g., '90 days').
findings.impact: Lack of documented log retention complicates breach investigation and incident response. GDPR Article 32 and ISO 27001 A.5.11 expect documented audit logging.

findings.title: CSP allows overly broad AWS API Gateway endpoint pattern
findings.description: CSP includes connect-src https://*.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com, allowing connections to any API Gateway endpoint in the region. This is broader than necessary and could allow requests to unintended endpoints if an attacker compromises the site's JavaScript context.
findings.fix_suggestion: Restrict CSP to the specific API endpoint: connect-src 'self' https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com https://plausible.io; If the endpoint domain is managed externally, document why the wildcard is necessary.
findings.evidence: CSP at line 66: connect-src 'self' https://*.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com https://plausible.io; The wildcard allows any endpoint in the region.
findings.impact: Overly permissive CSP reduces protection value. Low risk for a static portfolio site but represents unnecessary attack surface.

findings.title: No documented Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)
findings.description: GDPR Article 30 requires controllers to maintain a Record of Processing Activities. While small sites may have simple records, there is no evidence of a formal RoPA document in the repository or accompanying documentation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Create a simple Record of Processing Activities (internal document, not public) listing: (1) Contact form: name/email/message, purpose: respond to inquiry, retention: 30-90 days, basis: consent, no sharing. (2) Plausible analytics: aggregated usage, purpose: understand traffic, retention: per Plausible policy, basis: consent or legitimate interest, processor: Plausible.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy describes processing in narrative form but no structured RoPA document (CSV, table, or JSON) listing data categories, purposes, retention, recipients, and legal basis is visible.
findings.impact: Lack of formal RoPA does not automatically violate GDPR, but it weakens accountability and makes audits harder. Regulators expect to see a RoPA during enforcement actions.

findings.title: Missing privacy policy and data processing disclosure
findings.description: The pe-bootstrap web UI collects personal data (requestor name, scenario description, console password, deployment preferences) but provides no privacy policy, data processing notice, or transparency about how data is handled. Users are not informed of their data subject rights, retention periods, or legal basis for processing. A privacy policy is required by GDPR Art. 13 (pre-collection disclosure) and Art. 14 (post-collection disclosure if data wasn't directly collected from the data subject).
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Create a privacy policy page (e.g., frontend/web/privacy.html) that discloses: (a) controller identity and contact details, (b) purposes of processing (infrastructure deployment, audit logging), (c) legal basis (Art. 6 — likely legitimate interest or contract), (d) categories of personal data collected, (e) retention periods, (f) data subject rights and how to exercise them, (g) recipients if data is shared. 2. Link the privacy policy from the UI (footer or settings). 3. Add a notice on the deployment form explaining data handling. 4. Update API documentation with data handling expectations.
findings.evidence: No privacy policy link in frontend UI. No disclosure in the deployment form about data collection, storage, or retention. Variables collected: requestor (Art. 4(1) personal data), scenario (may contain personal data), console_password (sensitive data).
findings.impact: GDPR violations can result in administrative fines up to €20 million or 4% of annual global turnover, whichever is higher. Failure to provide privacy notices violates Art. 13–14 and could trigger supervisory authority enforcement action.

findings.title: No documented lawful basis for personal data processing
findings.description: The system collects and processes personal data (requestor name, email from Google OAuth, scenario descriptions, console password) without establishing or documenting a lawful basis under GDPR Art. 6. The system does not explicitly ask for consent, disclose legitimate interests, or establish a contractual relationship. Without a clear lawful basis, the processing is unlawful.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Determine the lawful basis for each processing activity: Likely candidates are (a) Art. 6(1)(b) if pe-bootstrap is part of an employment or service contract, (b) Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interests (infrastructure management, security, operational need). 2. Document the basis clearly in the privacy policy. 3. If using legitimate interests, conduct and document a Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) showing that interests are not overridden by individual rights. 4. Update the terms of service or user agreement to reference the lawful basis. 5. For Google OAuth, clarify in the privacy policy that you are relying on consent (Art. 7) or another basis, and ensure users can withdraw consent.
findings.evidence: Auth token handling at line 630 accepts tokens without consent flow. Personal data stored in metadata (lines 690–715 in main.py) with no lawful basis notation. Google OAuth integration (lines 567–579) but no disclosure of data use or consent.
findings.impact: Processing personal data without a lawful basis is a material GDPR violation. Supervisory authorities can issue cease-and-desist orders and impose significant fines (up to €20 million or 4% of turnover).

findings.title: No mechanism for data subject access requests
findings.description: Users/requestors have no way to request and receive a copy of their personal data held by the system. GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to obtain a copy of their data within 30 days. The system stores requestor names, scenario descriptions, deployment metadata, and audit trails in GCS but provides no way for users to export or retrieve this data.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement a /api/user/data endpoint that returns all personal data associated with the authenticated user (deployment metadata, audit log entries, etc.). 2. Return data in a structured, machine-readable format (JSON or CSV). 3. Add a button in the web UI (e.g., under Settings or Account) that allows users to download their data. 4. Document the 30-day SLA for responding to requests.
findings.evidence: No API endpoint for data export (GET /api/user/data or similar). Metadata stored in GCS (line 403–414) is readable only by administrators with GCS access, not by end users.
findings.impact: Failure to respond to data subject access requests within 30 days violates GDPR Art. 15 and can result in enforcement orders and fines.

findings.title: No mechanism for data deletion or erasure
findings.description: While users can destroy their deployments, the system does not provide a way to delete the associated personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment records) from the system. GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure (except where data must be retained for legal/contractual reasons). Metadata remains in GCS indefinitely after deployment destruction.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Implement a delete endpoint /api/deployments/{deployment_id}/delete that (a) verifies the authenticated user owns the deployment, (b) deletes metadata, outputs, and audit logs from GCS, (c) returns a confirmation. 2. Create a retention policy: offer a 30-day grace period before hard deletion, then purge all data. 3. Add a 'delete my data' option in the web UI. 4. Document the retention period in the privacy policy.
findings.evidence: destroy_deployment endpoint (line 792) destroys GCP infrastructure but does not delete metadata or audit logs from GCS. Metadata remains in GCS at deployments/{deployment_id}/metadata.json indefinitely.
findings.impact: Failure to honor erasure requests violates GDPR Art. 17 and can result in enforcement orders and fines. Prolonged retention of personal data without justification violates Art. 5 (storage limitation).

findings.title: No documented data retention policy
findings.description: The system collects and stores personal data (requestor metadata, audit trails, deployment outputs) with no documented retention policy or automatic cleanup mechanism. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The system appears to retain deployment metadata indefinitely.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Define a data retention policy: (a) Deployment metadata: retain for 90 days post-deployment-destruction, then delete. (b) Audit trails: retain for 1 year for compliance/security purposes, then delete. 2. Implement automated deletion via GCP Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Functions or GCS lifecycle rules. 3. Document the retention policy in the privacy policy and CLAUDE.md. 4. Add a /api/user/data-retention endpoint that discloses the retention schedule to users.
findings.evidence: Metadata is written to GCS (line 403) and read indefinitely. A TTL field exists in metadata (line 709) but it appears to be informational only (for deployment lifecycle, not data retention). No cleanup job or lifecycle policy is enforced on GCS blobs.
findings.impact: Indefinite retention of personal data violates the storage limitation principle (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e)) and can result in enforcement action. Regulators expect documented, justified retention periods.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e), Art. 25 (Storage Limitation & Data Protection by Design)

findings.title: Missing security headers and TLS enforcement
findings.description: The web UI does not implement critical HTTP security headers (Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy) that are required by GDPR Art. 32 (security measures commensurate with the level of risk). Absence of these headers increases vulnerability to XSS, clickjacking, and data interception.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add security headers to Flask responses via middleware or after_request hook: (a) Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload (b) Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://accounts.google.com; img-src 'self' data: (c) X-Frame-Options: DENY (d) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (e) Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (f) Cache-Control: private, no-store for sensitive endpoints. 2. Restrict CORS to specific origins (not wildcard). 3. Enforce HTTPS redirects.
findings.evidence: Flask app (line 24) does not set security headers. CORS(app) at line 25 is configured without explicit origin restrictions. No Content-Security-Policy or HSTS header configuration observed.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 32 requires appropriate technical and organizational measures, including encryption and integrity verification. Missing security headers violate this requirement and could be cited in breach notifications. Attackers could intercept data or perform XSS attacks, exposing personal data.

findings.title: Overly permissive CORS configuration
findings.description: CORS is enabled with no explicit origin restrictions (CORS(app) enables all origins). This allows any website to make requests to the API on behalf of authenticated users, increasing the risk of cross-site request forgery (CSRF) and unauthorized access if an attacker can trick a user into visiting a malicious site while logged in.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Replace CORS(app) with explicit origin whitelisting: from flask_cors import CORS; CORS(app, resources={'/api/*': {'origins': ['https://pe-bootstrap.example.com']}}) 2. Identify the frontend domain(s) that will call the API and whitelist only those. 3. Set credentials=True and allow_headers=['Authorization', 'Content-Type'].
findings.evidence: CORS(app) at line 25 with no origin restrictions. frontend/infra/main.tf comment acknowledges 'Cloud Run is publicly accessible (CORS requires requests to reach Flask)' but does not restrict to legitimate origins.
findings.impact: Overly permissive CORS violates GDPR Art. 32 security requirements. A breach via CORS misconfiguration could expose deployment metadata and audit logs, triggering breach notification obligations.

findings.title: No mechanism to correct inaccurate personal data
findings.description: Users cannot correct inaccurate requestor names or scenario descriptions after deployment creation. GDPR Art. 16 grants the right to rectification of inaccurate data. The system should allow users to update these fields.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add a PATCH /api/deployments/{deployment_id} endpoint that allows users to update: scenario, requestor name. 2. Verify user ownership of the deployment before allowing updates. 3. Log all changes in the audit trail. 4. Add an edit button in the web UI (e.g., in the deployment details page).
findings.evidence: No PATCH /api/deployments/{id} endpoint to update metadata. Metadata is write-once after creation (line 717).
findings.impact: Failure to provide a way to correct inaccurate personal data violates GDPR Art. 16 and contributes to inaccuracy violations under Art. 5(1)(d).

findings.title: Audit logs stored without integrity protection
findings.description: Audit logs are appended to JSON files in GCS (append_audit_event, line 436) but there is no cryptographic proof of integrity or tamper-detection. An attacker with GCS write access could modify logs to hide their actions. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires audit logs to be protected against unauthorized access and modification.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Migrate audit logging to Google Cloud Logging (Stackdriver) instead of JSON files in GCS. Cloud Logging provides immutable, tamper-proof audit trails. 2. Configure log retention (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security-relevant logs) via log sinks. 3. Set up log analysis/alerting for suspicious events (multiple failed auth attempts, unauthorized API calls). 4. Ensure logs are accessible to security and compliance teams but not to end users.
findings.evidence: Audit events are appended to metadata.json in GCS (lines 441–451). The file is mutable and can be overwritten by anyone with GCS write permission. No checksums, signatures, or immutable storage (e.g., Cloud Logging) used.
findings.impact: Lack of log integrity violates ISO 27001 A.8.15 and GDPR Art. 32 requirements for secure processing. In the event of a breach, logs may be considered unreliable evidence, hampering breach investigation and regulatory response.

findings.title: Session tokens stored in browser sessionStorage (sensitive)
findings.description: Authentication tokens (passphrase or Google ID token) are stored in browser sessionStorage (app.js line 82, 34), which is readable by any JavaScript on the page. While sessionStorage is cleared on browser close (better than localStorage), it is still vulnerable to XSS attacks. A malicious script can read sessionStorage and steal the token.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Store authentication tokens in memory only (not in sessionStorage), or use HttpOnly cookies if possible. 2. Implement rate limiting and account lockout on failed login attempts to mitigate brute-force attacks. 3. Set CSP header to restrict script sources (reduces XSS risk). 4. Consider adding a token expiration mechanism (e.g., tokens expire after 1 hour, requiring re-authentication). 5. Investigate using Web Crypto API for in-memory token management.
findings.evidence: sessionStorage.setItem('pe_auth_token', token) at line 82 and sessionStorage.getItem at line 9. No HttpOnly flag or secure storage mechanism.
findings.impact: XSS vulnerability leading to token theft could allow attackers to impersonate users and access deployments, violating ISO 27001 A.8.2 (authentication) and GDPR Art. 32 (security). Breached tokens could expose sensitive deployment data.

findings.title: No rate limiting on authentication endpoints
findings.description: The /api/auth/verify endpoint accepts unlimited auth attempts without rate limiting, enabling brute-force attacks against the passphrase. An attacker could try thousands of passphrases per second until one succeeds.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add rate limiting to /api/auth/verify: implement exponential backoff (e.g., 5 attempts per minute, then 1 attempt per 5 minutes after 5 failed attempts). 2. Use Flask-Limiter library: from flask_limiter import Limiter; limiter.limit('5/minute')(auth_verify). 3. Log repeated failures and alert on suspicious patterns. 4. Consider implementing account lockout: temporary lock after N failed attempts.
findings.evidence: auth_verify endpoint (line 625) has no rate-limiting decorator or logic. No mention of rate limiting in the Flask app configuration.
findings.impact: Brute-force attacks could compromise the web UI, allowing unauthorized access to deployment management, violating ISO 27001 A.8.3 and GDPR Art. 32 security requirements.

findings.title: Limited input validation on requestor name
findings.description: The requestor field is validated only for non-emptiness and presence of valid characters (line 189), but no length limits or content restrictions are enforced. A malicious requestor could submit extremely long strings or special characters that could cause issues in logging or downstream systems.
findings.fix_suggestion: 1. Add maximum length validation: len(requestor) <= 255. 2. Reject non-ASCII or control characters if unexpected. 3. Add validation for scenario field similarly.
findings.evidence: validate_params checks requestor with: if not requestor.strip() and if not slugify_label(requestor), but does not enforce a maximum length. slugify_label (line 297) trims to 63 chars, but validation doesn't check length before slugification.
