findings.impact: Input validation bypasses could lead to log injection, DoS, or data corruption. While not directly a GDPR violation, it contributes to system instability that could cause data loss or unauthorized access.

findings.title: Missing data export mechanism for right to data portability
findings.description: GDPR Article 20 requires that data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The system has account deletion but no data export endpoint that allows users to download their data (profile info, settings, saved content, notes, journals, bookmarks, plans, preferences, etc.) in a portable format like JSON or CSV.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement GET /api/export endpoint that: (1) authenticates the requesting user, (2) queries all user data from DynamoDB using the user ID, (3) assembles data into a JSON or CSV format with all personal data fields, (4) returns the export as a downloadable file. Include: user profile, all studies, all journals, all bookmarks, preferences, plan progress, streaks, and event-related data. Add a timestamp to the export showing when it was generated. Implement rate limiting to prevent abuse.
findings.evidence: The API routes show /api/account DELETE endpoint at line 540, but no GET /api/export or similar endpoint. The handleDeleteAccount function (line 1051) deletes all user data but does not provide export capability. User data includes: profile (name, email), studies, journals, bookmarks, daily verses, plans, streak data, preferences, event intake data.
findings.impact: GDPR violations can result in fines up to €20 million or 4% of global annual revenue, whichever is higher. Non-compliance with data portability rights specifically triggers supervisory authority investigations and user complaints. Users in EU may file complaints with national Data Protection Authorities.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval for scripts
findings.description: The CSP header in CloudFront allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script-src, which defeats the purpose of CSP and significantly increases the attack surface for Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injection attacks. While unsafe-inline is sometimes necessary for SPA applications, the addition of unsafe-eval is particularly dangerous and should be avoided.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Remove 'unsafe-eval' immediately — there is almost never a legitimate reason for unsafe-eval. (2) For unsafe-inline: attempt to remove by using Content Security Policy nonces or hashes for inline scripts. If that's not feasible due to Next.js architecture, document why it's necessary and document compensating controls (e.g., strong input validation, output encoding). (3) Test that existing scripts still work with a stricter CSP (using test policy first). (4) Consider using a CSP violation reporting mechanism to catch injection attempts.
findings.evidence: Line 278: content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' https://plausible.io; ..." The script-src directive explicitly includes both 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval', which allow arbitrary JavaScript execution and bypass CSP protections.
findings.impact: XSS vulnerabilities can lead to: account hijacking, data theft, malware injection. Not directly a GDPR violation but compromises confidentiality and integrity of personal data. ISO 27001 A.8.24 requires cryptographic and logical controls on data. CSP weakening is a control failure. Incident response would be required if XSS leads to data breach.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Cryptography & Key Management) and OWASP Top 10 (A03:2021 Injection)

findings.title: No granular consent mechanism for non-essential cookies and analytics
findings.description: The privacy policy mentions data collection and processing, but there is no cookie consent banner or granular consent mechanism visible on the site. The login page checks for GPC (Global Privacy Control) signal, but this is browser-level rather than site-specific consent. Third-party analytics (plausible.io is mentioned in CSP) may be loaded without explicit user consent. Without proper consent mechanisms, any non-essential cookies or analytics tracking violates GDPR requirements for lawful basis.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement a cookie consent solution: (1) Add a consent banner on first load (non-intrusive but visible) that allows users to: accept essential cookies only, or accept all including analytics/marketing. (2) Store consent choices in localStorage and HttpOnly cookie with a 12-month expiry. (3) Only load plausible.io analytics script if user consents. (4) Create a preference center (linked from footer) allowing users to change consent at any time. (5) Document in privacy policy what cookies are used and for what purpose. (6) Implement consent records in the database (which user consented to what, when, which privacy policy version).
findings.evidence: Login page (line 43) detects GPC signal but does not show an opt-in consent banner for analytics. CSP (line 278) allows plausible.io connections (analytics). Privacy policy mentions 'Technical and security data such as IP address, browser metadata, rate-limit events, and operational logs' but does not show a granular consent flow. No consent cookie or preference center visible in codebase.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 6(1)(a) requires explicit consent for processing based on consent. Without granular consent, any analytics/marketing data processing is unlawful. Fines up to €10 million or 2% of revenue. Supervisory authorities actively enforce this via monitoring for missing consent banners.

findings.title: Operational log retention policy not implemented or documented
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' but there is no evidence of implemented data deletion or TTL mechanism in the codebase. DynamoDB table has a TTL attribute (expiresAt) but it appears to be used only for rate-limit entries and password reset codes, not for audit/operational logs. Without automated cleanup, logs could be retained indefinitely.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Add explicit TTL to all DynamoDB log entries: set expiresAt = current_time + 30 days when logging. (2) For S3 access logs, configure S3 Lifecycle Policy to delete objects after 30 days. (3) For CloudWatch Logs, set log group retention to 30 days. (4) For any application-level logging (e.g., to external service), implement rotation and deletion. (5) Implement a data retention policy document and review logs quarterly. (6) Ensure logging does not capture sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII); if needed, hash or redact before logging.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB TTL is configured for the 'expiresAt' attribute (line 38-41) but logging calls at line 295-301 use console.log and do not set an expiresAt value. CloudFront access logs (terraform/cloudfront.tf line 132-135) are logged to S3 with no expiration policy visible. AWS CloudWatch logs (likely used for Lambda) have no retention policy visible in Terraform.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. Indefinite retention of logs (especially containing IP addresses, user IDs, request metadata) violates storage limitation. Regulatory exposure if audit shows logs retained beyond stated 30-day period.

findings.title: Logs may contain sensitive data without documented sanitization
findings.description: The logging function (logAuthEvent at line 295-301) logs to console without explicitly excluding sensitive fields. While passwords are hashed in the database, other sensitive information like email addresses, user IDs, and request metadata are logged. The password reset code is stored in plaintext in DynamoDB (line 755), and error messages in API responses may leak information about user existence (though email enumeration is partially protected).
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Define a logging policy: document which fields are safe to log (timestamps, event names, anonymized/hashed identifiers). (2) Implement log sanitization: before logging any field, check against a 'safe fields' whitelist. (3) Hash or redact PII before logging. (4) Hash password reset codes in DynamoDB (use timing-safe comparison on verification like passwords). (5) Test that logs do not contain passwords, tokens, or plaintext PII. (6) Document retention and access controls for logs (who can view, from where).
findings.evidence: Line 295-301: logAuthEvent logs timestamps and event names with details object that may contain userId, emailHash, and reason. Line 609-610 and 690-691 hash the email before logging but still reveal existence of authentication attempts. Line 755 stores reset code in plaintext (should be hashed). No documented policy on what fields are safe to log.
findings.impact: If logs are breached, exposed PII violates GDPR Art. 32 (security of processing) and Art. 33 (breach notification). Exposed email addresses + failed login attempts enable targeted phishing. ISO 27001 A.8.15 requires logging of access and changes; A.8.16 requires audit trail protection.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15 & A.8.16 (Logging & Audit Trails) and A.8.25 (Input Validation)

findings.title: Multi-factor authentication not implemented despite security claims
findings.description: The README and privacy policy describe the system as secure and trustworthy, but there is no MFA support. In a system handling event organizer data and attendee information (PII), MFA should be available for users with administrative access or handling sensitive event data. Current single-factor authentication (password + email verification) is insufficient for a B2B platform.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Implement optional TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) using libraries like speakeasy or similar. (2) Add API endpoints: POST /api/auth/mfa/setup (returns QR code), POST /api/auth/mfa/verify (validates TOTP), POST /api/auth/mfa/disable (requires current password). (3) Require MFA for admin accounts (ADMIN_EMAILS set at line 16-18). (4) Add MFA state to token (mfaVerified flag). (5) After login, if MFA is enabled, require TOTP verification before returning full access token. (6) Provide backup recovery codes for account access if authenticator app is lost.
findings.evidence: Login handler (line 669-718) performs password verification and returns token without any MFA challenge. No routes for MFA setup, verification, or enforcement visible in the API routes (lines 425-520). Login page has no MFA input fields.
findings.impact: Weak authentication increases risk of unauthorized access to organizer event data, attendee contact information, and booth/vendor data. While not directly a GDPR violation, compromised accounts lead to breaches. ISO 27001 A.8.2 recommends MFA for sensitive systems. Regulatory bodies increasingly expect MFA for business-critical accounts.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment documented for event data processing
findings.description: The system processes personal data of event organizers, attendees, speakers, vendors, and booth managers. This includes names, emails, event attendance information, photos (gallery feature), and potentially sensitive role/access information. GDPR Art. 35 requires a DPIA where processing is likely to result in high risk. While the README mentions the app stores 'event and organizer data such as saved local notes, preview settings, bookmarks, and preferences,' there's no documented DPIA or risk assessment.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Conduct a DPIA covering: event photo gallery (collection of attendee photos), role-based access (tracking who has admin/organizer permissions), event attendance tracking, email marketing (event reminders), third-party data sharing (if any). (2) Document: data flow, purposes, retention, recipients, rights of data subjects, technical/organizational safeguards, risks, and mitigations. (3) Store DPIA in a protected document (not public) but be prepared to share with supervisory authorities if requested. (4) If high-risk processing is found, document consultation with DPO if applicable. (5) Review DPIA annually or when processing significantly changes.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists data collected but does not mention DPIA or specific risk mitigations for event data. README (lines 43-72) describes various data collected but no mention of privacy impact assessment. No DPIA document visible in docs/.
findings.impact: If processing is high-risk and no DPIA is conducted, GDPR Art. 35 is violated. Supervisory authorities specifically check for DPIAs when investigating complaints about high-risk processing (e.g., photo galleries, role-based access, event attendance tracking).
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default) & Art. 35 (DPIA)

findings.title: Backup and disaster recovery procedures not documented
findings.description: While DynamoDB has point-in-time recovery enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf line 43-45), there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objectives (RTO), recovery point objectives (RPO), or disaster recovery runbook. No mention of backup testing or verification procedures. Incident response procedures are not documented in the codebase.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Document RTO and RPO: define acceptable downtime and data loss (e.g., RTO 4 hours, RPO 1 hour). (2) Enable DynamoDB Exports to S3 on a daily schedule (via Lambda + CloudWatch Events). (3) Implement S3 cross-region replication for critical backups. (4) Document backup retention: keep 7 days of daily exports, 4 weeks of weekly exports. (5) Test restore procedures quarterly and document results. (6) Create incident response runbook: breach (immediate steps, notification, investigation), data loss (restore from backup), service outage (failover plan). (7) Assign incident commander and escalation contacts.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB has PITR enabled but no S3 backup, DynamoDB Exports, or multi-region replication visible. README and CLAUDE.md do not mention backup or disaster recovery procedures. No runbook for incident response (breach, service outage, data corruption) visible.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires backup and recovery capability. GDPR breach notification (Art. 33) requires documented incident response with specific timelines (72 hours). Without documented procedures, response times will be slow and recovery may not meet user expectations (e.g., attendees unable to access event during outage).
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.13 (Backup & Recovery) and A.5.28 (Incident Response)

findings.title: Cache-Control headers not explicitly set for sensitive endpoints
findings.description: CloudFront default cache behavior (terraform/cloudfront.tf line 159-182) does not explicitly set Cache-Control headers for sensitive pages like login, account settings, and data views. Browser caching of sensitive content could expose user data if the browser cache is accessed by another user on a shared computer.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Add Cache-Control header to CloudFront response headers policy for sensitive paths (/login, /settings, /e/*/manage, /account). (2) For these paths: Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate, max-age=0. (3) For public/marketing pages (/features, /pricing, /contact): Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600. (4) Test that sensitive pages are not cached by inspecting response headers. (5) Document cache policy in security documentation.
findings.evidence: CloudFront default_cache_behavior sets TTLs (min 0, default 86400, max 31536000) but does not add Cache-Control headers to the response. The next.config.ts does not configure response headers. API responses (line 128) only set Content-Type and CORS headers.
findings.impact: User data cached in browser history/cache could be exposed if computer is shared. Not a direct GDPR/ISO violation but a data security risk. Affects confidentiality of personal data on shared devices (e.g., library, office shared computer).
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Security Headers) and A.8.32 (Change Management)

findings.title: Admin functionality not restricted by role-based access control
findings.description: Admin access is determined by checking email membership in a hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS set (lambda/api/index.mjs line 16-18) containing only 'cody@codyjo.com'. This is not scalable and lacks audit trail. There is no granular role-based access control (RBAC) system. As the system grows and additional staff need access, admin privileges cannot be delegated or revoked via UI.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Create a ROLES table: id, userId, role (admin, organizer, viewer, etc.), grantedAt, grantedBy, revokedAt. (2) Replace hardcoded ADMIN_EMAILS with database query: queryCommand for USER#X/ROLE. (3) Add admin UI: list users, grant/revoke roles, view audit log. (4) Log all role changes and admin actions: CREATE event with adminUserId, targetUserId, action, before/after state. (5) Implement role-based permission checks for all sensitive endpoints. (6) Document roles and their capabilities. (7) Require re-authentication for sensitive admin operations (password prompt).
findings.evidence: Line 16-18: ADMIN_EMAILS hardcoded set with only one email. Line 219-225: isAdmin and requireAdmin functions check membership. No UI or database-driven role assignment visible. No audit log of admin actions.
findings.impact: Lack of granular access control violates ISO 27001 A.8.5 (access control) principle. If admin credentials are compromised, all data is at risk. No ability to audit who made what change (violates A.8.16 audit trails for changes).
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.5 (Access Control) and A.5.9 (Principle of Least Privilege)

findings.title: JWT secret rotation policy not documented
findings.description: JWT secret is stored in AWS Secrets Manager (lambda/api/index.mjs line 25-36) and cached after cold start. There is no documented rotation policy. If the secret is compromised, old tokens remain valid until expiry (30 days per line 178). Lack of key rotation guidance increases risk of long-term compromise.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) Document JWT secret rotation policy: rotate every 90 days (or immediately if compromise suspected). (2) Implement key versioning in Secrets Manager: store both current and previous secrets, try current first, fall back to previous if verification fails. (3) Create a rotation Lambda triggered by EventBridge on a 90-day schedule. (4) Shorter token expiry: consider 1-7 days instead of 30 days, requiring refresh tokens for longer sessions. (5) Implement token revocation list (denylist) for compromised tokens or explicit logout. (6) Document rotation procedure and test it in a dev environment.
findings.evidence: getJwtSecret() caches the secret after first retrieval (line 24). Token expiry is hardcoded to 30 days (line 178: exp: now + 86400 * 30). No key rotation Lambda or procedure visible.
findings.impact: If JWT secret is compromised, attacker can forge tokens valid for 30 days. ISO 27001 A.8.24 recommends key rotation. GDPR Art. 32 requires technical measures appropriate to the risk.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Key Management) and A.5.12 (Access Control)

findings.title: Privacy information not provided for event attendees registered by organizers
findings.description: The system allows event organizers to import or register attendees (guest list, booth manager endpoints visible). When an attendee is registered by an organizer (not self-registration), the attendee may not receive privacy information about how their data is processed. GDPR Art. 14 requires disclosure of controller identity, purposes, recipients, rights, etc. when data is collected indirectly.
findings.fix_suggestion: (1) When an organizer adds an attendee to an event (guest list, booth), send an email to the attendee with: controller identity (Cordivent), purposes (event coordination, attendance tracking), retention period, rights (access, deletion, portability). (2) Include a link to the full privacy policy and unsubscribe link. (3) Log that notification was sent. (4) Provide organizers with a template/guidance on what privacy info to include when manually inviting attendees. (5) In-app notification option for attendees already in the system: notify them that they were added to [event name] and link to privacy info.
findings.evidence: API routes at line 434-435 show /api/events/[slug]/register and /api/events/[slug]/self-checkin endpoints. Guest list and booth manager features allow organizers to add attendees. No evidence of automatic privacy notice being sent to attendees added by organizers.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 14 violation if attendees are not informed when added to events. Supervisory authority would expect documentation that privacy info was provided, either via email or in-app notification.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 14 (Information to be provided where personal data are not obtained from the data subject)

gdpr.notes: Privacy policy is comprehensive and user-facing, but critical data subject rights mechanisms are incomplete. Right to data portability missing entirely. Consent mechanisms insufficient for non-essential processing.

iso27001.notes: Strong authentication and encryption infrastructure. Password hashing uses industry best practices (PBKDF2). Security headers well-configured with one notable exception (unsafe-inline in CSP). Logging and incident response procedures not documented.

age_verification.notes: Project is B2B event management platform with business/organizer focus. Not directed to children. Age verification laws do not apply to this use case.

findings.title: Unclear lawful basis for processing reading interpretations and analytics
findings.description: While registration requires consent to the privacy policy, the privacy policy does not clearly articulate the specific lawful basis for each processing activity. The policy mentions sending reading context to Anthropic (Claude API) as 'automated decision-making support you explicitly request', but does not establish a clear legal basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, etc.) for collecting and processing analytics data (IP address, browser metadata, rate-limit events). Art. 6 requires a lawful basis for every processing activity.
findings.fix_suggestion: Revise the privacy policy to explicitly state the lawful basis for each major processing activity: (1) Account data and readings: Contract (Art. 6(1)(b)) as necessary for service provision; (2) Analytics: Legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)) - specify the legitimate interests being served; (3) Email communications: Consent for marketing, contract for transactional. Create a detailed processing activity table or data mapping document. Ensure GDPR Art. 13 transparency for all collection points.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'Why we use it' includes 'To generate readings and operate the features you explicitly request', but does not distinguish between processing bases for different activities. Analytics collection (IP, browser metadata) lacks explicit basis disclosure.
findings.impact: GDPR violations can result in fines up to 4% of annual turnover or €20 million. Lack of clear lawful basis for all processing is a foundational compliance gap that could expose the organization to enforcement action by supervisory authorities.

findings.title: No age verification or parental consent mechanism for users under 16
findings.description: The privacy policy explicitly states 'intended only for people who are at least 16 years old' and 'not directed to children under 13', but there is no age gate, age verification, or parental consent mechanism at registration. A user can register with any age and the system does not verify or restrict access. Under GDPR Art. 8, processing of children's data under 16 requires parental consent (in most EU member states). Under COPPA (for US users), children under 13 require verifiable parental consent. Under California's AADC (AB 2273), businesses likely accessed by children must implement age-appropriate design and protections. The absence of enforcement allows minors to create accounts and have their data processed without proper safeguards.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement one of the following: (1) Age gate at registration requiring DOB entry and verification (reject users under 16); (2) Parental consent flow for users 13-16 (requires email verification from parent); (3) Age estimation using third-party service (Yoti, AgeChecker, etc.). For US users, implement COPPA compliance: age verification, parental consent for <13, verifiable parental email. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA. Consider geographic detection to apply jurisdiction-specific rules. Add prominent age disclaimer on login/registration pages.
findings.evidence: handleRegister function accepts registration from any user without age verification. No age gate UI component exists. Privacy policy (src/app/privacy/page.tsx:99-100) states age requirement in text form only.
findings.impact: GDPR violations for processing children's data without parental consent: up to 4% of turnover. COPPA violations: up to $43,280 per violation. CCPA/AADC violations: up to $2,500-$7,500 per violation. Enforcement action by FTC, state AGs, and EU supervisory authorities. Loss of user trust and reputational damage.
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (15 U.S.C. § 6501-6506), California AADC (AB 2273)

findings.title: No documented Data Processing Agreements with third-party processors
findings.description: The privacy policy discloses that reading context is sent to Anthropic (Claude API) for interpretation and that AWS and Resend are used for hosting and email. However, there is no evidence of documented Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) in place with these vendors. Under GDPR Art. 28, any processor who handles personal data on behalf of a controller must have a written DPA in place that specifies: processor obligations, instructions for data processing, confidentiality, security measures, data subject rights support, and subprocessor authorization. The absence of DPAs is a direct violation of GDPR.
findings.fix_suggestion: Obtain or review DPAs from: (1) Anthropic for Claude API usage; (2) AWS for S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, CloudFront, Secrets Manager services; (3) Resend for email service. Ensure DPAs include: data subject request support, subprocessor notification, deletion/return of data on termination, audit rights, data security obligations. Create a Data Processing Register documenting all processors. Link DPA summaries in the privacy policy or provide on request.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states: 'When you request an AI interpretation or related AI feature, the relevant reading context may be processed by Anthropic to return that response. The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States, and transactional emails may use Resend where configured.' No DPA links or references present in code or README.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 28 violation: supervisory authority can issue corrective orders and fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of annual turnover (whichever is higher). Personal liability for data protection officers and controllers. Cannot demonstrate processor compliance to auditors or regulators.

findings.title: Incomplete disclosure of international data transfers and adequacy decisions
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States' but does not explicitly inform EU users that their data is transferred outside the EEA. GDPR Art. 13 requires controllers to inform data subjects of transfers outside the EEA and the legal mechanism ensuring adequate protection (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decision, Binding Corporate Rules). While the US is not designated as having adequate protection under GDPR, transfers can proceed under SCCs or other mechanisms. The privacy policy must inform users of this transfer and the safeguards in place.
findings.fix_suggestion: Revise privacy policy to include: (1) Explicit statement that personal data is transferred to and processed in the United States by AWS, Anthropic, and Resend; (2) Statement that the US is not deemed to have adequate data protection by the European Commission; (3) Explanation of safeguards: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) in place with US processors, compliance with Schrems II supplementary measures where applicable; (4) Link to Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) or summary; (5) EU users' rights to request supplementary safeguards; (6) Contact information for data transfer inquiries. Ensure SCCs are in place with all US processors.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'The app is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States' but does not explain: (1) legal mechanism for transfer (SCCs, adequacy decision, etc.); (2) that EU users' data is stored and processed in the US; (3) Schrems II implications; (4) right to supplementary measures.
findings.impact: GDPR violation if adequate safeguards are not in place or not disclosed. Fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of turnover. Supervisory authority enforcement. Potential data subject claims for damages (Art. 82). User distrust.

findings.title: Missing explicit API rate limiting on resource-intensive endpoints
findings.description: While rate limiting is implemented for authentication endpoints (registration: 3/hour, login: 5/15min, account deletion: 5/15min), there is no evidence of rate limiting on resource-intensive API endpoints like POST /api/interpret (AI interpretation) and POST /api/readings/share (public share generation). These endpoints could be abused to: (1) Generate high numbers of API calls to Anthropic (cost and DoS); (2) Create numerous share tokens; (3) Exceed DynamoDB capacity. Without rate limiting, a malicious user can cause service degradation or unexpected costs.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting on POST endpoints: (1) POST /api/interpret: limit to 10 interpretations per user per hour; (2) POST /api/readings: limit to 20 readings per user per day; (3) POST /api/readings/share: limit to 5 shares per user per hour; (4) POST /api/journals: limit to 10 journals per user per day. Use the existing checkRateLimit() function pattern. Log rate limit events for monitoring. Consider using x-ratelimit headers in responses to inform clients of limits.
findings.evidence: Rate limiting is implemented for REGISTER, LOGIN, and account deletion via checkRateLimit() function, but handleShareReading (line 296), handleSaveReading, and other endpoints lack explicit rate limiting. No rate limiting logic found in index.mjs outside of auth handlers.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 violation (lack of access control and use limitation). Potential for DDoS or cost-based attacks. Operational risk: service unavailability, unexpected cloud costs, harm to users' service experience.

findings.title: Incomplete audit logging and missing security event coverage
findings.description: While the application logs some security events (auth_register_duplicate, data_export, auth_account_delete, auth_password_reset), audit logging is not comprehensive. Missing audit coverage includes: (1) Successful logins and login failures (only failed registrations logged); (2) Email changes and password changes (code has logging for delete, but grep confirms no logging for changePassword/changeEmail endpoints); (3) Journal access and reads; (4) Admin API access and actions; (5) Data modification events (reading creation, updates); (6) Encryption key generation/recovery. Audit trails must be immutable, timestamped, and retention policies defined.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand audit logging to cover all security-relevant events: (1) Authentication: successful login, failed login, password change, email change, account deletion; (2) Authorization: failed access attempts to protected endpoints, admin actions; (3) Data access: journal reads (consider if necessary), export data requests; (4) Encryption: key generation, key recovery attempts, key rotation; (5) Third-party integrations: AI interpretation requests sent to Anthropic; (6) Sensitive operations: account modifications, reading sharing. Log: event type, user ID, timestamp, IP address, result (success/failure). Do not log passwords, tokens, or sensitive data. Implement log retention policy (e.g., 90 days for operational logs, 1 year for security logs). Consider shipping logs to CloudWatch Logs with long-term S3 archival for immutability.
findings.evidence: Code shows console.log for specific events: 'auth_account_delete' (line 299), 'data_export', but missing logs for: successful logins, email changes, password changes (other than deleteAccount), admin actions, reading creation/modification.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 A.8.15/A.8.16 non-compliance. Inability to detect security incidents or trace unauthorized access. Breach detection and investigation hindered. Regulatory bodies expect comprehensive audit logs for compliance audits.
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15, A.8.16 (Logging and Audit Trails)

findings.title: Vague data retention policy for operational logs
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review' (README.md: line 89). This is vague and does not meet GDPR transparency requirements. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. The policy should specify: (1) exact retention periods for different data categories; (2) how retention is enforced (TTL, scheduled cleanup, manual deletion); (3) when 'longer retention for security review' is triggered; (4) what happens after retention period expires.
findings.fix_suggestion: Establish and document explicit retention schedules: (1) Operational logs (auth, API errors): 30 days auto-expiry via CloudWatch Logs retention; (2) Security-relevant logs (failures, deletions, exports): 90 days; (3) Billing/audit logs: 7 years (for regulatory/tax purposes); (4) User data (readings, journals): indefinite until user deletion. Implement retention via CloudWatch Logs retention policies, DynamoDB TTL, and S3 lifecycle policies. Document in privacy policy and operational runbooks. Update Terraform to enforce retention.
findings.evidence: README states: 'Operational logs are generally retained for about 30 days unless needed longer for security review.' No explicit retention schedule, TTL configuration, or cleanup job found in code. CloudWatch Logs retention not configured in Terraform.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) violation: storage limitation principle not met. Fines up to 10 million euros or 2% of annual turnover. Data subjects cannot trust retention policies.

findings.title: Potential exposure of sensitive data in logs
findings.description: While the application is careful not to log full tokens or passwords, the current logging approach logs user IDs and IP addresses for audit purposes. If logs are exposed or accessed by unauthorized parties, these can be correlated to identify users. Additionally, CloudWatch Logs and application error responses may expose sensitive information: (1) Error messages may leak internal API structure or backend details; (2) Exception stack traces in development might expose code paths; (3) CloudWatch Logs are accessible to IAM-permitted users without end-to-end encryption.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement comprehensive log handling: (1) Mask or hash sensitive data in logs: user IDs can be hashed, IP addresses can be anonymized (truncate last octet); (2) Use structured logging (JSON) for easier parsing and filtering; (3) Implement log access controls: restrict CloudWatch Logs access to authorized personnel via IAM; (4) Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption at rest (already available in AWS); (5) Set log retention for CloudWatch Logs to 30-90 days; (6) Never log request/response bodies containing sensitive data; (7) Use AWS VPC endpoints to route logs securely; (8) Document log access and audit logs.
findings.evidence: Logging statements include userId and IP (line 299: 'userId: user.userId, ip: event.requestContext?.http?.sourceIp'). Error response (line 299) returns generic 'Internal server error' (good), but detailed error logs go to CloudWatch. No mention of log encryption or access controls in Terraform.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 non-compliance. Potential data breach if logs are exposed. GDPR risk if user IDs and IPs are logged without strong access controls and retention limits. Reputational damage.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design considerations for minor users
findings.description: While the app is 'not directed to children', if minors access the app, there are no design safeguards to protect their interests. GDPR Art. 8 and California AADC (AB 2273) require age-appropriate design for services likely to be used by children. This includes: (1) Default high-privacy settings for minors; (2) No dark patterns (infinite scroll, autoplay, addictive design); (3) Prominent, accessible privacy information in language suitable for children; (4) No profiling or behavioral targeting of minors; (5) Restrictions on data sharing with third parties. The app has no age-specific safeguards.
findings.fix_suggestion: If the app is intentionally not targeting minors, consider: (1) Adding prominent age gate and terms confirming 16+ access only; (2) If minor access is anticipated, implement age-appropriate mode: high-privacy defaults, disable data sharing, no behavioral tracking, simplified UI with large text, accessible privacy information; (3) Parental dashboard for minors 13-16 (parental approval required); (4) Restrict features available to minors (e.g., no public sharing, limits on data export); (5) No marketing or promotional content to minors. Document compliance in privacy policy and DPIA.
findings.evidence: No age detection logic in code. Settings page treats all users equally (no 'minor mode'). UI design does not include age-specific protections. No parental controls or guardian approval flows.
findings.impact: GDPR Art. 8 and AADC (AB 2273) non-compliance if minors use the service. Fines up to €20 million or 4% of turnover (GDPR). FTC enforcement and state AG fines for COPPA/AADC violations ($2,500-$7,500 per violation).
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, COPPA (Children's Online Privacy Protection Act)

findings.title: Incomplete documentation of backup and disaster recovery procedures
findings.description: The DynamoDB configuration includes point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled, which is excellent. However, there is no documented backup strategy, recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), or tested recovery procedures. ISO 27001 A.8.13 requires documented backup and recovery procedures with regular testing.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document and implement: (1) Backup strategy: DynamoDB PITR (current), daily full backups to S3, cross-region replication for failover; (2) Define RTO and RPO: RTO <4 hours, RPO <1 hour; (3) Recovery procedures: documented runbooks for DynamoDB recovery, S3 data recovery, Lambda redeployment; (4) Test recovery quarterly: execute full restore from backup to test environment; (5) Communicate backup and recovery procedures to ops team; (6) Monitor backup success via CloudWatch alarms; (7) Document in runbooks and architecture documentation.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB PITR enabled (line 38-40), but no backup strategy, RTO/RPO, or disaster recovery documentation found in code comments, README, or ops documentation. No backup testing mentioned.
findings.impact: ISO 27001 non-compliance. Risk of data loss in case of major incident. Service unavailability. Reputational damage.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline scripts (acceptable but verbose)
findings.description: The Content Security Policy (CSP) in Terraform (cloudfront.tf, line 235) includes 'script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io'. While 'unsafe-inline' is necessary for Plausible analytics initialization and is from a trusted third party, it reduces the effectiveness of CSP against XSS attacks. Additionally, the CSP allows inline styles ('style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline''), which could be exploited. This is a minor issue but represents a defense-in-depth opportunity.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider: (1) Moving Plausible initialization to an external script file to remove need for 'unsafe-inline'; (2) Using Content Security Policy nonces for any unavoidable inline scripts; (3) Restricting style-src to 'self' only and using CSS classes instead of inline styles; (4) Adding script-src hash if Plausible init code is fixed. For now, 'unsafe-inline' is acceptable given the trusted nature of Plausible, but evaluate in future security reviews.
findings.evidence: CSP policy includes: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' https://plausible.io" and "style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'". While inline scripts are minimized in the codebase, this relaxation of CSP weakens XSS protection.
findings.impact: Minor ISO 27001 non-compliance. Slight increase in XSS risk if inline script injection vulnerabilities are present in dependencies.
